title: PI-JEPA: Label-Free Surrogate Pretraining for Coupled Multiphysics Simulation via Operator-Split Latent Prediction
authors: Brandon Yee; Pairie Koh
principal_argument: Label-free pretraining on unlabeled reservoir parameter fields can substantially reduce the labeled simulation budget for multiphysics neural surrogates.
methodology: PI-JEPA uses masked latent prediction on unlabeled parameter fields with per-sub-operator PDE residual regularization, with a predictor bank aligned to Lie-Trotter operator splitting and separate latent modules for pressure, saturation transport, and reaction before fine-tuning on limited labeled runs.
main_findings: On single-phase Darcy flow, PI-JEPA reports 1.9x lower error than FNO and 2.4x lower error than DeepONet at 100 labeled runs, plus a 24% gain over supervised-only training at 500 labeled runs.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract-level evidence is centered on single-phase Darcy flow and low-label fine-tuning settings, so broader multiphysics generalization is not established here.
future_opportunities: Test the pretraining scheme on broader multiphysics operators and more realistic reservoir simulation settings.

title: Mine-JEPA: In-Domain Self-Supervised Learning for Mine-Like Object Classification in Side-Scan Sonar
authors: Taeyoun Kwon; Youngwon Choi; Hyeonyu Kim; Myeongkyun Cho; Junhyeok Choi; Moon Hwan Kim
principal_argument: Carefully designed in-domain self-supervised learning can outperform far larger general-purpose vision foundation models for side-scan sonar mine classification under data scarcity.
methodology: Mine-JEPA pretrains with the SIGReg self-supervised loss on 1,170 unlabeled sonar images, then evaluates binary and 3-class mine-like object classification, including a compact ViT-Tiny setting and synthetic data augmentation.
main_findings: The method reaches F1 0.935 for mine vs. non-mine and 0.820 for 3-class classification with augmentation, outperforming fine-tuned DINOv3 in both settings; applying in-domain SSL to foundation models degrades performance by 10 to 13 points, and the compact backbone remains competitive with 4x fewer parameters.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary is limited to side-scan sonar classification with a small unlabeled corpus and binary/3-class tasks.
future_opportunities: Evaluate the approach on broader sonar datasets and clarify when domain adaptation helps or harms stronger pretrained models.

title: JEPA-MSAC: A Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Multimodal Sensing-Assisted Communications
authors: Can Zheng; Jiguang He; Guofa Cai; Nannan Li; Mehdi Bennis; Henk Wymeersch; Merouane Debbah
principal_argument: A shared self-supervised multimodal predictive backbone can support several wireless PHY tasks with lower adaptation cost than task-specific supervised pipelines.
methodology: JEPA-MSAC maps multimodal sensing and communication measurements into a unified token space, pretrains a shared backbone with temporal block-masked JEPA, then freezes that backbone and trains lightweight heads for localization, beam prediction, and RSSI prediction.
main_findings: The learned latent state supports accurate multi-task prediction with low adaptation cost, and ablations highlight scaling behavior and the effect of key pretraining choices.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not provide headline numerical gains and focuses on a specific set of wireless prediction tasks.
future_opportunities: Extend the framework to more sensing modalities, communication settings, and downstream PHY tasks.

title: Le MuMo JEPA: Multi-Modal Self-Supervised Representation Learning with Learnable Fusion Tokens
authors: Ciem Cornelissen; Sam Leroux; Pieter Simoens
principal_argument: Learnable fusion-token bottlenecks can make multimodal JEPA training efficient while preserving strong downstream performance across aligned sensor pairs.
methodology: Le MuMo JEPA extends LeJEPA with modality-specific patch stems, a shared transformer, learnable fusion tokens, pruned fusion after early cross-modal attention, and SIGReg on the joint multimodal CLS embedding; it is tested on RGB-depth and RGB-thermal settings.
main_findings: The model gives the strongest performance-efficiency trade-off among from-scratch multimodal baselines on Waymo, remains strongest under from-scratch training on nuScenes, and achieves the best FLIR results, especially after Waymo-initialized fine-tuning, while using less compute and memory.
limitations_or_gaps: The demonstrated scope is aligned RGB-depth and RGB-thermal perception benchmarks, so broader modality mixes are not covered in the summary.
future_opportunities: Apply the fusion-token design to additional sensor combinations and transfer settings beyond driving benchmarks.

title: Var-JEPA: A Variational Formulation of the Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture -- Bridging Predictive and Generative Self-Supervised Learning
authors: Moritz Gögl; Christopher Yau
principal_argument: JEPA can be cast as a variational latent-variable model, and making that structure explicit yields a more principled predictive self-supervised objective with uncertainty estimates.
methodology: Var-JEPA derives a single-ELBO formulation from coupled latent-variable models, replacing heuristic anti-collapse regularization with an explicit variational objective; the paper instantiates this as Var-T-JEPA for tabular data.
main_findings: The framework produces meaningful representations without ad hoc anti-collapse regularizers, enables principled latent uncertainty quantification, and consistently improves over T-JEPA on tabular tasks while staying competitive with strong raw-feature baselines.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstracted evidence is for tabular data, so effectiveness on vision, audio, or video settings is not established here.
future_opportunities: Extend the variational JEPA formulation to other modalities and study uncertainty-aware downstream uses.

title: V-JEPA 2.1: Unlocking Dense Features in Video Self-Supervised Learning
authors: Lorenzo Mur-Labadia; Matthew Muckley; Amir Bar; Mido Assran; Koustuv Sinha; Mike Rabbat; Yann LeCun; Nicolas Ballas; Adrien Bardes
principal_argument: Dense predictive objectives, hierarchical self-supervision, multimodal tokenization, and scale can substantially improve JEPA-style visual world modeling for images and videos.
methodology: V-JEPA 2.1 combines a dense masking-based predictive loss over visible and masked tokens, deep supervision across intermediate encoder layers, unified tokenizers for images and videos, and scaling in data and model size.
main_findings: The model achieves state-of-the-art benchmark results including 7.71 mAP on Ego4D short-term object-interaction anticipation, 40.8 Recall@5 on EPIC-KITCHENS high-level action anticipation, and a 20-point gain in real-robot grasping success over V-JEPA-2 AC, alongside strong navigation, depth, and recognition performance.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary emphasizes benchmark wins on the studied image, video, and robotics tasks without detailing failure modes or narrower deployment constraints.
future_opportunities: Scale the dense-feature recipe further and apply it to additional embodied, video-understanding, and robot-perception tasks.

title: Is the reconstruction loss culprit? An attempt to outperform JEPA
authors: Alexey Potapov; Oleg Shcherbakov; Ivan Kravchenko
principal_argument: JEPA may not owe its advantage purely to avoiding reconstruction loss; objective asymmetries and predictable-component selection also matter, and gated predictive autoencoders can surpass it on the studied toy system.
methodology: The paper compares JEPA-style predictive learning and reconstruction autoencoders on a controlled linear dynamical-system toy problem, performs diagnostic analyses with PCA baselines, and introduces gated predictive autoencoders that select predictable components.
main_findings: The apparent JEPA robustness advantage to noise weakens under closer diagnostics, and the proposed gated predictive autoencoder is stable across noise levels while matching or outperforming JEPA on the toy benchmark.
limitations_or_gaps: Results are explicitly confined to a toy linear dynamical system rather than real-world high-dimensional datasets.
future_opportunities: Test whether the gated predictive-autoencoder idea transfers to more realistic nonlinear representation-learning settings.

title: Sat-JEPA-Diff: Bridging Self-Supervised Learning and Generative Diffusion for Remote Sensing
authors: Kursat Komurcu; Linas Petkevicius
principal_argument: Combining JEPA-style semantic prediction with diffusion generation can better balance structural fidelity and texture realism in satellite-image forecasting.
methodology: Sat-JEPA-Diff uses an IJEPA module to predict stable semantic representations and conditions a frozen Stable Diffusion backbone through a lightweight cross-attention adapter, with evaluation on a global Sentinel-2 dataset.
main_findings: The method produces sharper boundaries, attains leading perceptual scores including GSSIM 0.8984 and FID 0.1475, and outperforms deterministic baselines despite standard autoregressive stability limits.
limitations_or_gaps: The reported evidence is tied to Sentinel-2 remote sensing and the summary still notes autoregressive stability limits.
future_opportunities: Assess the approach on more remote-sensing regimes and further improve long-horizon stability.

title: LeWorldModel: Stable End-to-End Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture from Pixels
authors: Lucas Maes; Quentin Le Lidec; Damien Scieur; Yann LeCun; Randall Balestriero
principal_argument: End-to-end JEPA world models can be trained stably from pixels with a very simple objective, avoiding the heavy stabilization machinery used by prior approaches.
methodology: LeWorldModel trains from raw pixels using only a next-embedding prediction loss and a Gaussian latent regularizer, with roughly 15M parameters on a single GPU, then evaluates control performance plus physical probing and surprise detection.
main_findings: The method is presented as the first JEPA to train stably end-to-end from raw pixels with only two loss terms, cuts tunable loss hyperparameters from six to one versus the only existing end-to-end alternative, plans up to 48x faster than foundation-model-based world models, and remains competitive across varied 2D and 3D control tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract-level evidence is centered on control benchmarks and does not establish performance across broader world-modeling domains.
future_opportunities: Apply the simplified end-to-end JEPA recipe to additional environments and downstream planning or anomaly-detection settings.

title: Social-JEPA: Emergent Geometric Isomorphism
authors: Haoran Zhang; Youjin Wang; Yi Duan; Rong Fu; Dianyu Zhao; Sicheng Fan; Shuaishuai Cao; Wentao Guo; Xiao Zhou
principal_argument: Independent agents can learn latent spaces that become approximately isometric when trained predictively on the same world, enabling direct cross-agent transfer.
methodology: Separate agents learn world models from different viewpoints without coordination or parameter sharing; the paper then analyzes latent-space geometry, zero-update classifier transfer, and distillation-like migration between agents.
main_findings: The learned latent spaces exhibit an approximate linear isometry despite large viewpoint shifts and limited pixel overlap, allowing classifier transfer without extra gradient steps and reducing later learning compute through migration.
limitations_or_gaps: The setup focuses on agents observing the same environment from different viewpoints, so broader interoperability claims remain limited at the abstract level.
future_opportunities: Probe whether the emergent alignment persists across more heterogeneous agents, tasks, and environments.

title: Semantic Tube Prediction: Beating LLM Data Efficiency with JEPA
authors: Hai Huang; Yann LeCun; Randall Balestriero
principal_argument: A JEPA-style geometric regularizer for language hidden states can dramatically improve LLM data efficiency beyond standard scaling-law expectations.
methodology: The paper introduces the Geodesic Hypothesis and Semantic Tube Prediction, which constrains hidden-state trajectories to a tubular neighborhood around a semantic geodesic, serving as a JEPA-style regularizer without explicit multi-view augmentations.
main_findings: Semantic Tube Prediction improves signal-to-noise ratio, preserves diversity by preventing trajectory collisions during inference, and matches baseline accuracy with 16x less training data on NL-RX-SYNTH.
limitations_or_gaps: The reported evidence is on NL-RX-SYNTH, so broader language benchmarks and model scales are not covered in the summary.
future_opportunities: Test the geometric regularizer on wider LLM workloads and study how semantic-manifold priors interact with scaling laws.

title: Knowledge, Rules and Their Embeddings: Two Paths towards Neuro-Symbolic JEPA
authors: Yongchao Huang; Hassan Raza
principal_argument: JEPA can bridge statistical learning and symbolic reasoning by both injecting rule structure into predictive representations and relaxing rules into continuous spaces for generation and discovery.
methodology: The proposed RiJEPA framework adds Energy-Based Constraints and a multimodal dual-encoder to encode logical biases, then uses differentiable rule relaxations with gradient-guided Langevin diffusion for continuous rule discovery, conditional generation, and inference.
main_findings: Empirical evaluations on synthetic topological simulations and a clinical use case are reported to confirm the framework's efficacy and support robust, generative, and interpretable neuro-symbolic representation learning.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary does not report quantitative results and only names synthetic and one clinical use case as evidence.
future_opportunities: Validate the framework on broader neuro-symbolic domains and further study scalable rule discovery and interpretability.

title: US-JEPA: A Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture for Medical Ultrasound
authors: Ashwath Radhachandran; Vedrana Ivezić; Shreeram Athreya; Ronit Anilkumar; Corey W. Arnold; William Speier
principal_argument: For ultrasound, predicting masked latent targets from a frozen domain-specific teacher offers a more stable and efficient self-supervised route than reconstruction or EMA-teacher JEPA variants.
methodology: US-JEPA applies the SALT objective with a frozen domain-specific teacher, decoupling student and teacher optimization, and benchmarks representation quality through linear probing across multiple UltraBench classification tasks against available ultrasound foundation models.
main_findings: US-JEPA achieves competitive or superior linear-probe results relative to domain-specific and universal vision foundation model baselines, supporting masked latent prediction as a stable path for ultrasound representation learning.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract centers on UltraBench linear-probe classification, so broader downstream clinical tasks are not detailed.
future_opportunities: Extend the frozen-teacher JEPA approach to more ultrasound tasks and other medical-imaging settings.

title: JEPA-DNA: Grounding Genomic Foundation Models through Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Ariel Larey; Elay Dahan; Amit Bleiweiss; Raizy Kellerman; Guy Leib; Omri Nayshool; Dan Ofer; Tal Zinger; Dan Dominissini; Gideon Rechavi; Nicole Bussola; Simon Lee; Shane O'Connell; Dung Hoang; Marissa Wirth; Alexander W. Charney; Nati Daniel; Yoli Shavit
principal_argument: Adding JEPA-style latent grounding to genomic pretraining can capture higher-level functional context that MLM or next-token prediction alone tends to miss.
methodology: JEPA-DNA combines standard generative objectives with latent-space supervision on a CLS token so the model predicts functional embeddings of masked genomic segments; it can be used from scratch or as continual pretraining for existing genomic foundation models.
main_findings: Across diverse genomic benchmarks, JEPA-DNA consistently outperforms generative-only baselines on supervised and zero-shot tasks and yields more biologically grounded representations.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary does not break out benchmark-specific results and is specific to genomic sequence modeling.
future_opportunities: Apply latent grounding to additional biological-sequence models and more genomic foundation-model pipelines.

title: GOT-JEPA: Generic Object Tracking with Model Adaptation and Occlusion Handling using Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: Shih-Fang Chen; Jun-Cheng Chen; I-Hong Jhuo; Yen-Yu Lin
principal_argument: Predicting tracking models rather than only image features can improve generic object tracking generalization and fine-grained occlusion handling.
methodology: GOT-JEPA uses a teacher predictor to generate pseudo-tracking models from a clean current frame and trains a student to match them from a corrupted frame given the same history; it is paired with OccuSolver for object-aware visibility estimation and iterative occlusion refinement.
main_findings: The framework provides stable pseudo supervision under occlusions, distractors, and adverse observations, while OccuSolver improves visibility labels and occlusion handling; evaluations on seven benchmarks indicate stronger generalization and robustness.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not provide headline numerical improvements and focuses specifically on generic object tracking and occlusion reasoning.
future_opportunities: Extend predictive tracking-model pretraining and iterative occlusion reasoning to broader tracking conditions and scene dynamics.

title: Self-Supervised JEPA-based World Models for LiDAR Occupancy Completion and Forecasting
authors: Haoran Zhu; Anna Choromanska
principal_argument: A self-supervised JEPA world model can learn useful LiDAR representations for modeling spatiotemporal scene evolution in autonomous driving.
methodology: Introduces AD-LiST-JEPA, a JEPA-based world model trained on unlabeled LiDAR data, and evaluates the learned encoder on LiDAR occupancy completion and forecasting.
main_findings: Proof-of-concept experiments show that JEPA pretraining improves downstream occupancy completion and forecasting performance.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary reports proof-of-concept results centered on the OCF downstream task; broader long-horizon planning validation is not described.
future_opportunities: Scale self-supervised LiDAR world-model pretraining and test whether the learned representations improve longer-horizon planning and driving tasks.

title: Intrinsic-Energy Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures Induce Quasimetric Spaces
authors: Anthony Kobanda; Waris Radji
principal_argument: Intrinsic JEPA energy functions form quasimetrics, linking JEPA representation learning with quasimetric reinforcement learning and asymmetric goal-reaching dynamics.
methodology: Presents a theoretical analysis of intrinsic least-action JEPA energies under closure and additivity assumptions, and compares them with goal-reaching cost-to-go functions.
main_findings: Shows that any intrinsic energy is a quasimetric, that optimal cost-to-go functions admit this intrinsic form, and that symmetric finite energies mismatch one-way reachability.
limitations_or_gaps: This is a short theoretical article; the summary does not describe empirical validation beyond the conceptual connection.
future_opportunities: Use asymmetric intrinsic-energy JEPA objectives in goal-conditioned control and reinforcement-learning settings where directionality matters.

title: JEPA-VLA: Video Predictive Embedding is Needed for VLA Models
authors: Shangchen Miao; Ningya Feng; Jialong Wu; Ye Lin; Xu He; Dong Li; Mingsheng Long
principal_argument: Video-predictive embeddings, especially V-JEPA 2, address key weaknesses in existing visual representations for vision-language-action models.
methodology: Analyzes shortcomings of current VLA visual representations, then introduces JEPA-VLA to adaptively integrate predictive video embeddings into existing VLAs and evaluates it on simulation and real-robot benchmarks.
main_findings: Predictive embeddings better capture task-relevant temporal dynamics and JEPA-VLA delivers substantial gains on LIBERO, LIBERO-plus, RoboTwin2.0, and real-robot tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary emphasizes benchmark gains but does not detail compute trade-offs, failure modes, or settings where predictive embeddings may be less helpful.
future_opportunities: Integrate video-predictive pretraining more broadly into robotic policy learning to improve sample efficiency and generalization.

title: Causal-JEPA: Learning World Models through Object-Level Latent Interventions
authors: Heejeong Nam; Quentin Le Lidec; Lucas Maes; Yann LeCun; Randall Balestriero
principal_argument: Object-level masking in JEPA-style world models induces a causal inductive bias that improves interaction reasoning and planning efficiency.
methodology: Proposes C-JEPA, which extends masked joint embedding prediction from patches to object-centric representations using object-level masking, and evaluates it on visual question answering and control tasks alongside a formal analysis.
main_findings: C-JEPA improves counterfactual reasoning by about 20% absolutely, reaches comparable control with only 1% of the latent input features of patch-based models, and is supported by a formal causal analysis.
limitations_or_gaps: The approach depends on object-centric representations, and the summary only reports results on the listed VQA and control settings.
future_opportunities: Apply object-level latent interventions to broader world-model, reasoning, and control problems where interactions matter.

title: VLA-JEPA: Enhancing Vision-Language-Action Model with Latent World Model
authors: Jingwen Sun; Wenyao Zhang; Zekun Qi; Shaojie Ren; Zezhi Liu; Hanxin Zhu; Guangzhong Sun; Xin Jin; Zhibo Chen
principal_argument: Leakage-free latent prediction lets VLA pretraining focus on action-relevant state transitions rather than pixel variation or nuisance motion.
methodology: Introduces VLA-JEPA, where a target encoder provides latent targets from future frames while the student sees only the current observation, followed by action-head fine-tuning.
main_findings: Across LIBERO, LIBERO-Plus, SimplerEnv, and real-world manipulation tasks, VLA-JEPA improves robustness and generalization over prior methods.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary highlights benchmark gains but does not describe detailed ablations, limitations, or behavior outside the reported manipulation settings.
future_opportunities: Extend leakage-free JEPA pretraining to broader robotic policies and internet-scale video learning pipelines.

title: Online Monitoring Framework for Automotive Time Series Data using JEPA Embeddings
authors: Alexander Fertig; Karthikeyan Chandra Sekaran; Lakshman Balasubramanian; Michael Botsch
principal_argument: JEPA-based self-supervised embeddings can support online anomaly monitoring for automotive time-series data without anomaly labels.
methodology: Builds a JEPA-based self-supervised prediction task to learn object-state embeddings, then applies established anomaly-detection methods to those embeddings and evaluates on nuScenes.
main_findings: The learned JEPA embeddings provide expressive latent representations that enable anomaly identification in real-world automotive data.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary focuses on object-state monitoring and nuScenes-based experiments, without describing broader deployment results or labeled anomaly comparisons.
future_opportunities: Deploy the framework in live vehicle monitoring pipelines and test it on a wider range of unknown anomaly types and operational conditions.

title: BiJEPA: Bi-directional Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture for Symmetric Representation Learning
authors: Yongchao Huang
principal_argument: Bi-directional JEPA with cycle consistency can learn more symmetric representations than standard uni-directional JEPA when stabilized appropriately.
methodology: Proposes BiJEPA with bi-directional prediction and a critical norm regularization mechanism, then evaluates it on synthetic periodic signals, Lorenz attractors, and MNIST.
main_findings: BiJEPA converges stably without collapse, captures semantic structure in chaotic systems, and learns robust representations that support generation and generalisation.
limitations_or_gaps: The reported evaluation is limited to synthetic and benchmark datasets in the summary, rather than larger real-world applications.
future_opportunities: Test bidirectional predictive objectives on more complex modalities and larger-scale representation-learning problems.

title: MTS-JEPA: Multi-Resolution Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Time-Series Anomaly Prediction
authors: Yanan He; Yunshi Wen; Xin Wang; Tengfei Ma
principal_argument: A multi-resolution JEPA with a soft codebook bottleneck can capture anomaly precursors across time scales while avoiding collapse.
methodology: Introduces MTS-JEPA, combining a multi-resolution predictive objective with a soft codebook bottleneck that separates transient shocks, long-term trends, and regime transitions.
main_findings: The proposed design prevents degenerate solutions and achieves state-of-the-art results under the early-warning protocol on standard benchmarks.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary reports benchmark performance but does not discuss deployment constraints or behavior beyond the evaluated early-warning setting.
future_opportunities: Apply multi-resolution JEPA anomaly prediction to critical infrastructure monitoring and other multivariate time-series domains.

title: A Lightweight Library for Energy-Based Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Basile Terver; Randall Balestriero; Megi Dervishi; David Fan; Quentin Garrido; Tushar Nagarajan; Koustuv Sinha; Wancong Zhang; Mike Rabbat; Yann LeCun; Amir Bar
principal_argument: A lightweight, modular library can make energy-based JEPA methods accessible across image, video, and action-conditioned world-model settings.
methodology: Presents the EB-JEPA open-source library with single-GPU examples, plus ablations on CIFAR-10, a Moving MNIST video example, and an action-conditioned Two Rooms world-model task.
main_findings: The library demonstrates 91% probing accuracy on CIFAR-10, scalable multi-step video prediction, 97% planning success on Two Rooms, and the importance of regularization for avoiding collapse.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary emphasizes lightweight examples and educational accessibility rather than broad production-scale benchmarks.
future_opportunities: Use the library as a base for research, teaching, and more extensive experiments on JEPA representation learning and world models.

title: Cell-JEPA: Latent Representation Learning for Single-Cell Transcriptomics
authors: Ali ElSheikh; Rui-Xi Wang; Weimin Wu; Yibo Wen; Payam Dibaeinia; Jennifer Yuntong Zhang; Jerry Yao-Chieh Hu; Mei Knudson; Sudarshan Babu; Shao-Hua Sun; Aly A. Khan; Han Liu
principal_argument: Predicting latent cell embeddings from partial observations can learn dropout-robust single-cell representations better than masked count reconstruction.
methodology: Introduces Cell-JEPA, which predicts cell-level embeddings from partial gene observations, and evaluates zero-shot cell-type clustering and perturbation prediction.
main_findings: Cell-JEPA improves zero-shot transfer clustering over scGPT and improves absolute-state reconstruction in perturbation prediction, though not effect-size estimation.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary explicitly notes that effect-size estimation does not improve, indicating incomplete gains for perturbation modeling.
future_opportunities: Combine JEPA-based representation learning with stronger perturbation-modeling methods for more complete cellular prediction.

title: Rectified LpJEPA: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures with Sparse and Maximum-Entropy Representations
authors: Yilun Kuang; Yash Dagade; Tim G. J. Rudner; Randall Balestriero; Yann LeCun
principal_argument: JEPA regularization should support sparse, non-negative, maximum-entropy representations rather than only dense Gaussian-like ones.
methodology: Introduces Rectified Distribution Matching Regularization to align representations with a Rectified Generalized Gaussian distribution, yielding Rectified LpJEPA.
main_findings: Rectified LpJEPA learns sparse, non-negative representations with strong sparsity-performance trade-offs and competitive image-classification performance.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary focuses on image-classification benchmarks, so broader modality coverage or downstream impacts are not described.
future_opportunities: Explore sparsity-controlled JEPA objectives in additional tasks and modalities where efficient representations may matter.

title: Drive-JEPA: Video JEPA Meets Multimodal Trajectory Distillation for End-to-End Driving
authors: Linhan Wang; Zichong Yang; Chen Bai; Guoxiang Zhang; Xiaotong Liu; Xiaoyin Zheng; Xiao-Xiao Long; Chang-Tien Lu; Cheng Lu
principal_argument: Combining V-JEPA pretraining with multimodal trajectory distillation can better align end-to-end driving representations with planning and behavioral ambiguity.
methodology: Adapts V-JEPA to large-scale driving videos, then adds a proposal-centric planner that distills simulator-generated and human trajectories with momentum-aware selection.
main_findings: On NAVSIM, the V-JEPA representation plus a simple decoder beats prior work in the perception-free setting, and the full Drive-JEPA system sets a new state of the art.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary reports benchmark results on NAVSIM, but does not discuss broader real-world deployment or other driving benchmarks.
future_opportunities: Extend predictive video pretraining and multimodal trajectory distillation to broader autonomous-driving planning systems.

title: Hierarchical JEPA Meets Predictive Remote Control in Beyond 5G Networks
authors: Abanoub M. Girgis; Ibtissam Labriji; Mehdi Bennis
principal_argument: Hierarchical JEPA can improve scalable predictive remote control by operating directly in compact embedding space under bandwidth limits.
methodology: Proposes a three-level hierarchical predictor over low-dimensional embeddings for different temporal resolutions, with control actions derived in latent space rather than reconstructed state space.
main_findings: In inverted cart-pole simulations, H-JEPA supports up to 42.83% more devices under limited wireless capacity without sacrificing control performance.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary only reports simulation results on inverted cart-pole systems, leaving broader system validation open.
future_opportunities: Evaluate hierarchical JEPA control in more complex wireless networked control scenarios and with richer device observations.

title: A Latent Space Framework for Modeling Transient Engine Emissions Using Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Ganesh Sundaram; Tobias Gehra; Jonas Ulmen; Mirjan Heubaum; Daniel Görges; Michael Günthner
principal_argument: A structured JEPA latent space can model transient engine emissions more accurately and efficiently than monolithic neural baselines.
methodology: Learns emission dynamics from combined PEMS and high-frequency hardware-in-the-loop data using a JEPA framework, then supports pruning and post-training quantization for deployment.
main_findings: The approach outperforms strong LSTM baselines in data efficiency, predictive accuracy, and generalization, while quantization and pruning greatly reduce footprint with negligible loss.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary is specific to transient engine-emission modeling and does not describe results beyond the reported datasets and regimes.
future_opportunities: Use the efficient JEPA model for onboard emission control, model predictive control, or model-based reinforcement learning in future powertrains.

title: RadJEPA: Radiology Encoder for Chest X-Rays via Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: Anas Anwarul Haq Khan; Mariam Husain; Kshitij Jadhav
principal_argument: Robust radiology image encoders can be learned from unlabeled chest X-rays without language supervision by predicting masked-region latent representations.
methodology: Introduces RadJEPA, a self-supervised JEPA framework pretrained solely on unlabeled chest X-rays and evaluated on disease classification, semantic segmentation, and report generation.
main_findings: RadJEPA outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, including Rad-DINO, across the reported radiology benchmarks.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary centers on chest X-rays, so extension to other medical imaging modalities is not established here.
future_opportunities: Apply language-free JEPA pretraining to broader radiology and medical-imaging representation-learning tasks.

title: VJEPA: Variational Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures as Probabilistic World Models
authors: Yongchao Huang
principal_argument: Deterministic JEPA objectives obscure probabilistic semantics and limit use in stochastic control, so a variational probabilistic JEPA can provide uncertainty-aware world modeling without reconstructing observations.
methodology: Introduces Variational JEPA to learn a predictive distribution over future latent states with a variational objective, develops Bayesian JEPA with a product-of-experts belief factorization, proves links to PSRs/Bayesian filtering and control sufficiency, and tests the approach in a noisy environment experiment.
main_findings: The paper claims VJEPA unifies JEPA-style representation learning with predictive state representations and Bayesian filtering, can avoid collapse, and in noisy experiments filters nuisance distractors better than generative baselines while enabling uncertainty estimates and modular constraint handling.
limitations_or_gaps: Evidence described in the abstract is centered on theory plus a noisy-environment experiment; broader empirical validation across more control domains is not reported there.
future_opportunities: Apply probabilistic JEPA world models to broader uncertainty-aware planning problems and further explore zero-shot transfer or modular constraint satisfaction with the Bayesian extension.

title: Video Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures for Facial Expression Recognition
authors: Lennart Eing; Cristina Luna-Jiménez; Silvan Mertes; Elisabeth André
principal_argument: V-JEPA pretraining can support facial expression recognition by learning semantically useful video embeddings without relying on pixel-level reconstruction.
methodology: Uses a pre-trained V-JEPA video encoder and trains shallow classifiers on the RAVDESS and CREMA-D datasets, with additional cross-dataset evaluation.
main_findings: The abstract reports state-of-the-art performance on RAVDESS, the best vision-based result on CREMA-D (+1.48 WAR), and strong cross-dataset generalization.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence presented is limited to the RAVDESS and CREMA-D FER benchmarks with shallow classifiers; wider task coverage is not discussed in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Extend embedding-based JEPA pretraining to broader FER benchmarks and related affective video understanding tasks.

title: HanoiWorld : A Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture BasedWorld Model for Autonomous Vehicle Controller
authors: Tran Tien Dat; Nguyen Hai An; Nguyen Khanh Viet Dung; Nguyen Duy Duc
principal_argument: A JEPA-based world model may offer a more safety-aware and effective alternative to data-hungry pixel-reconstruction reinforcement learning for autonomous vehicle control.
methodology: Introduces Hanoi-World, a JEPA-based world model with an RNN for long-term horizontal planning, and evaluates it in multiple Highway-Env simulation settings.
main_findings: The abstract says Hanoi-World can produce effective driving plans with safety awareness and achieves a favorable collision rate relative to stated baselines in the simulated environments.
limitations_or_gaps: The reported evidence is confined to Highway-Env simulations, and the abstract does not provide broader validation, detailed metrics, or real-world testing.
future_opportunities: Investigate JEPA-based planning for richer driving scenarios and study whether the safety-oriented world-model approach transfers beyond simulation.

title: BERT-JEPA: Reorganizing CLS Embeddings for Language-Invariant Semantics
authors: Taj Gillin; Adam Lalani; Kenneth Zhang; Marcel Mateos Salles
principal_argument: Adding a JEPA objective to BERT-style models can reorganize the CLS embedding space into more language-invariant semantic structure and improve multilingual performance.
methodology: Proposes BERT-JEPA, which augments BERT-style training with a JEPA objective targeting the CLS embedding space, and evaluates the result on multilingual benchmarks.
main_findings: The abstract states that the approach combats collapsed CLS embeddings, yields a language-agnostic embedding space, and improves results across multilingual benchmarks.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary does not specify the multilingual benchmarks or discuss behavior beyond BERT-style models.
future_opportunities: Explore JEPA-based objectives for multilingual representation learning in other language-model architectures and settings.

title: Value-guided action planning with JEPA world models
authors: Matthieu Destrade; Oumayma Bounou; Quentin Le Lidec; Jean Ponce; Yann LeCun
principal_argument: JEPA world models can support better action planning when their embedding geometry is explicitly shaped to reflect goal-conditioned value.
methodology: Introduces a training constraint so the negative goal-conditioned value function for reaching cost is approximated by distance between state embeddings, then compares the method with standard JEPA models on simple control tasks.
main_findings: The abstract reports significantly improved planning performance over standard JEPA models on the evaluated simple control tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: The empirical evidence mentioned is limited to simple control tasks, so broader planning settings are not established by the abstract alone.
future_opportunities: Test value-shaped JEPA representations in more complex control domains and broader model-based planning problems.

title: JEPA-Reasoner: Decoupling Latent Reasoning from Token Generation
authors: Bingyang Kelvin Liu; Ziyu Patrick Chen; David P. Woodruff
principal_argument: Reasoning should be decoupled from token generation so that latent reasoning is less vulnerable to expression errors and can better preserve uncertainty.
methodology: Proposes JEPA-Reasoner, which uses a JEPA module for latent-space reasoning and a separate Talker module for linguistic reconstruction, and evaluates the design in controlled synthetic and natural-language experiments.
main_findings: The abstract claims the decoupled design contains token-level errors, exposes the full latent reasoning trajectory to generation, supports mixed-hypothesis uncertainty, and yields a 149.5% 8-shot GSM8K improvement for a 0.9B model over a coupled Transformer baseline trained on the same data.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence described is limited to controlled experiments and a specific model scale; wider benchmark coverage is not detailed in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Pursue decoupled latent-reasoning architectures as an alternative scaling path for more robust complex reasoning systems.

title: VL-JEPA: Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture for Vision-language
authors: Delong Chen; Mustafa Shukor; Theo Moutakanni; Willy Chung; Jade Yu; Tejaswi Kasarla; Yejin Bang; Allen Bolourchi; Yann LeCun; Pascale Fung
principal_argument: Predicting continuous text embeddings rather than autoregressively generating tokens can make vision-language models more parameter-efficient while preserving or improving performance.
methodology: Builds a JEPA-based vision-language model that predicts target text embeddings, uses a lightweight decoder only when text output is needed, introduces selective decoding, and compares against standard token-space training under matched encoder and data conditions.
main_findings: The abstract reports stronger performance than standard token-space training with 50% fewer trainable parameters, 2.85x fewer decoding operations with similar quality under selective decoding, superior average results to CLIP, SigLIP2, and Perception Encoder on video classification and retrieval, and VQA performance comparable to classical VLMs with 1.6B parameters.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract highlights selected classification, retrieval, and VQA evaluations, but does not detail broader generative-language behavior or other deployment tradeoffs.
future_opportunities: Extend JEPA-style vision-language learning to richer multimodal tasks and further exploit adaptive decoding for efficient inference.

title: JEPA as a Neural Tokenizer: Learning Robust Speech Representations with Density Adaptive Attention
authors: Georgios Ioannides; Christos Constantinou; Aman Chadha; Aaron Elkins; Linsey Pang; Ravid Shwartz-Ziv; Yann LeCun
principal_argument: A JEPA-based latent prediction stage combined with density-adaptive attention can learn robust speech representations that also support efficient neural tokenization.
methodology: Uses a two-stage framework: first JEPA with density adaptive attention learns semantic audio features via masked latent prediction, then finite scalar quantization, mixed-radix packing, and a HiFi-GAN decoder produce reversible tokens and waveform reconstruction.
main_findings: The abstract says the method discovers hierarchical speech structure with adaptive temporal feature selection at 2.5 Hz and yields reversible compressed tokens at 47.5 tokens/sec that are competitive with and often more efficient than existing neural audio codecs.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not name benchmark datasets or downstream speech tasks, so empirical scope is only broadly described there.
future_opportunities: Develop language-model-friendly speech tokenizers and compressed audio representations building on JEPA-style latent speech learning.

title: CrossJEPA: Cross-Modal Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Efficient 3D Representation Learning from 2D Images
authors: Avishka Perera; Kumal Hewagamage; Saeedha Nazar; Kavishka Abeywardana; Hasitha Gallella; Ranga Rodrigo; Mohamed Afham
principal_argument: Cross-modal JEPA pretraining can turn 2D image supervision into an efficient route for strong 3D representation learning from point clouds.
methodology: Introduces CrossJEPA, which distills embeddings from a frozen image foundation model into a point-cloud encoder by predicting rendered 2D view embeddings, with cross-domain projection conditioning and cached target embeddings for efficiency.
main_findings: The abstract reports new state-of-the-art linear-probing accuracy on ModelNet40 (94.2%) and ScanObjectNN (88.3%) using 14.1M pretraining parameters and about six hours on a single GPU, while emphasizing memory and compute efficiency.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence emphasized in the abstract centers on linear probing over two 3D benchmarks; broader 3D tasks are not discussed there.
future_opportunities: Apply cross-modal JEPA distillation to additional 3D tasks and further exploit efficient frozen-teacher pretraining in resource-constrained settings.

title: DSeq-JEPA: Discriminative Sequential Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: Xiangteng He; Shunsuke Sakai; Shivam Chandhok; Sara Beery; Kun Yuan; Nicolas Padoy; Tatsuhito Hasegawa; Leonid Sigal
principal_argument: Sequentially predicting image regions in discriminative order can overcome the limitations of parallel I-JEPA prediction and yield stronger visual representations.
methodology: Proposes DSeq-JEPA, which uses attention-derived saliency to identify primary discriminative regions and then predicts later regions sequentially in a curriculum-like order, with evaluations across classification, fine-grained recognition, detection, segmentation, and reasoning tasks.
main_findings: The abstract states that DSeq-JEPA consistently learns more discriminative and generalizable representations than I-JEPA variants across the evaluated tasks.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary focuses on comparisons with I-JEPA variants on visual tasks and does not discuss possible computational tradeoffs in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Explore sequential discriminative prediction in broader self-supervised visual learning and reasoning settings.

title: Koopman Invariants as Drivers of Emergent Time-Series Clustering in Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Pablo Ruiz-Morales; Dries Vanoost; Davy Pissoort; Mathias Verbeke
principal_argument: JEPA time-series clustering can be explained by the predictive objective implicitly learning Koopman invariants, rather than by an unexplained emergent effect.
methodology: Develops a theoretical analysis showing when an idealized JEPA loss is minimized by regime-indicator representations tied to Koopman eigenfunctions, and validates the explanation on synthetic data with known dynamics.
main_findings: The abstract claims the theory explains regime clustering, identifies a near-identity linear predictor as the key inductive bias for learning invariants, and links predictor design to representation disentanglement.
limitations_or_gaps: Validation is described on synthetic data and an idealized theoretical setting, with no real-world time-series evaluation mentioned in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Use the Koopman-based perspective to design more interpretable and robust JEPA models for time-series analysis.

title: JEPA-T: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture with Text Fusion for Image Generation
authors: Siheng Wan; Zhengtao Yao; Zhengdao Li; Junhao Dong; Yanshu Li; Yikai Li; Linshan Li; Haoyan Xu; Yijiang Li; Zhikang Dong; Huacan Wang; Jifeng Shen
principal_argument: Late fusion plus objective-level text alignment can make a joint-embedding predictive transformer effective for text-conditioned image generation while keeping the backbone general.
methodology: Introduces JEPA-T, which encodes images and captions into discrete tokens, applies a joint-embedding predictive Transformer, adds cross-attention after the predictor for conditional denoising, injects raw text embeddings before the flow-matching loss, and uses iterative denoising at inference.
main_findings: On ImageNet-1K, the abstract reports strong data efficiency, open-vocabulary generalization, and consistent gains over non-fusion and late-fusion baselines.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract only names ImageNet-1K evaluations and does not describe broader text-to-image benchmarks or qualitative failure modes.
future_opportunities: Extend JEPA-style multimodal token modeling to broader text-to-image generation settings and other conditional generative tasks.

title: Rethinking JEPA: Compute-Efficient Video SSL with Frozen Teachers
authors: Xianhang Li; Chen Huang; Chun-Liang Li; Eran Malach; Josh Susskind; Vimal Thilak; Etai Littwin
principal_argument: EMA-updated teachers are not necessary for V-JEPA-style video self-supervision; a frozen teacher can provide a simpler and more compute-efficient alternative.
methodology: Introduces SALT, a two-stage scheme that first trains a target encoder by pixel reconstruction under V-JEPA masking, then freezes it and trains a student to predict the teacher latents on masked regions, followed by frozen-backbone benchmarking and scaling analysis.
main_findings: The abstract reports that SALT students outperform V-JEPA 2 encoders under frozen evaluation, achieve better accuracy-FLOPs tradeoffs, and remain strong even when the teacher is small or sub-optimal.
limitations_or_gaps: The evidence presented is framed around frozen-backbone evaluation and the two-stage setup; the abstract does not detail broader downstream or end-to-end finetuning behavior.
future_opportunities: Use frozen-teacher latent prediction for scalable video SSL and rethink compute allocation toward stronger students rather than more expensive teachers.

title: Self-supervised learning of imaging and clinical signatures using a multimodal joint-embedding predictive architecture
authors: Thomas Z. Li; Aravind R. Krishnan; Lianrui Zuo; John M. Still; Kim L. Sandler; Fabien Maldonado; Thomas A. Lasko; Bennett A. Landman
principal_argument: Multimodal JEPA pretraining on unlabeled longitudinal CT and EHR data can improve pulmonary nodule diagnosis, but its benefits may not transfer cleanly out of distribution.
methodology: Curates an unlabeled multimodal patient archive for JEPA pretraining, then performs supervised finetuning and compares against unregularized multimodal and imaging-only models on internal and external cohorts, alongside a synthetic environment for analyzing failure cases.
main_findings: The abstract reports improved internal-cohort AUC over both comparison models, but worse external-cohort AUC than the imaging-only model, highlighting both gains and generalization limits.
limitations_or_gaps: A key limitation is explicit in the abstract: the method underperforms on the external cohort, and the study is focused on one medical application and institution-specific unlabeled archive.
future_opportunities: Study when multimodal JEPA pretraining helps or hurts medical prediction and improve robustness to external distribution shifts.

title: Why and How Auxiliary Tasks Improve JEPA Representations
authors: Jiacan Yu; Siyi Chen; Mingrui Liu; Nono Horiuchi; Vladimir Braverman; Zicheng Xu; Dan Haramati; Randall Balestriero
principal_argument: Auxiliary tasks improve JEPA representations because they anchor the distinctions that latent dynamics alone might otherwise collapse.
methodology: Provides a theoretical analysis of a JEPA variant with a jointly trained auxiliary regression head, proves a no-unhealthy-collapse result in deterministic MDPs, and runs controlled ablations in a counting environment.
main_findings: The abstract claims that driving both transition-consistency and auxiliary losses to zero forces non-equivalent observations to remain distinct in latent space, and that joint training with the auxiliary head yields richer representations than separate training.
limitations_or_gaps: The formal result is stated for deterministic MDPs and the experiments described are in a counting environment, so broader stochastic settings are not established in the abstract.
future_opportunities: Design auxiliary objectives that encode the right equivalence relations for stronger JEPA encoders in model-based learning.

title: LLM-JEPA: Large Language Models Meet Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Hai Huang; Yann LeCun; Randall Balestriero
principal_argument: JEPA-style embedding-space objectives can improve LLM pretraining and finetuning over standard reconstruction-oriented language training objectives.
methodology: Introduces LLM-JEPA, a JEPA-based training approach for language models, and evaluates it across multiple datasets and model families in both pretraining and finetuning settings.
main_findings: The abstract reports significant gains over standard LLM objectives on NL-RX, GSM8K, Spider, and RottenTomatoes, with robustness to overfitting across Llama3, OpenELM, Gemma2, and Olmo models.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not specify detailed architectural trade-offs, compute cost, or settings where the JEPA objective may underperform.
future_opportunities: The work opens a path for broader JEPA-style training research for language models across additional tasks, scales, and training regimes.

title: Learning State-Space Models of Dynamic Systems from Arbitrary Data using Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Jonas Ulmen; Ganesh Sundaram; Daniel Görges
principal_argument: JEPA-style representation learning combined with continuous-time dynamics can recover structured latent state-space models from arbitrary observations.
methodology: Combines sequence embeddings with neural ODEs and uses losses enforcing contractive embeddings and Lipschitz-constrained state transitions; demonstrates the approach on a simple pendulum from image observations.
main_findings: The method is reported to generate structured latent state-space models for a pendulum using only image data, showing promise for world modeling from arbitrary observations.
limitations_or_gaps: Evidence in the abstract is limited to a simple pendulum example, with no detail on more complex systems, robustness, or downstream control performance.
future_opportunities: The abstract points to more general control and estimation methods in robotics built on these learned latent dynamics.

title: Improving Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture with Diffusion Noise
authors: Yuping Qiu; Rui Zhu; Ying-cong Chen
principal_argument: Injecting diffusion noise into JEPA-style masked image modeling can strengthen self-supervised visual representation learning.
methodology: Proposes N-JEPA, which incorporates diffusion noise through masked-token position embeddings and adds a multi-level noise schedule as a feature-augmentation strategy.
main_findings: A comprehensive downstream classification study is said to confirm the effectiveness of the method and the robustness benefits of the noise schedule.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract focuses on recognition performance and does not report detailed benchmark outcomes, generative behavior, or failure modes.
future_opportunities: The work suggests deeper exploration of the connection between diffusion-style corruption and self-supervised recognition models, alongside public code release.

title: From Video to EEG: Adapting Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture to Uncover Saptiotemporal Dynamics in Brain Signal Analysis
authors: Amirabbas Hojjati; Lu Li; Ibrahim Hameed; Anis Yazidi; Pedro G. Lind; Rabindra Khadka
principal_argument: Treating EEG as video-like sequences allows V-JEPA to learn scalable and interpretable spatiotemporal EEG representations for classification.
methodology: Adapts V-JEPA to EEG classification with joint embeddings and adaptive masking, and evaluates the model on the TUH Abnormal EEG dataset while examining learned signal patterns.
main_findings: EEG-VJEPA reportedly outperforms existing state-of-the-art EEG classifiers and captures physiologically relevant spatial and temporal patterns that support interpretability.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract highlights results on one public dataset and does not describe performance across broader EEG tasks, sites, or deployment conditions.
future_opportunities: The paper positions EEG-VJEPA as a candidate framework for scalable, trustworthy EEG analysis and human-AI support in diagnostic workflows.

title: Development and Comparative Evaluation of Three Artificial Intelligence Models (NLP, LLM, JEPA) for Predicting Triage in Emergency Departments: A 7-Month Retrospective Proof-of-Concept
authors: Edouard Lansiaux; Ramy Azzouz; Emmanuel Chazard; Amélie Vromant; Eric Wiel
principal_argument: A comparative triage study suggests LLM-based modeling is more effective than the tested NLP and JEPA alternatives for emergency-department triage prediction.
methodology: Evaluates three AI systems against the FRENCH triage scale and nurse practice using seven months of adult triage data from Roger Salengro Hospital in Lille, France.
main_findings: The LLM-based URGENTIAPARSE achieved the best reported accuracy, AUC-ROC, and hospitalization-prediction performance, outperforming both the NLP and JEPA models as well as nurse triage.
limitations_or_gaps: This is a seven-month retrospective proof-of-concept at a single hospital, and the abstract notes that adoption still depends on addressing limitations and ethical transparency.
future_opportunities: The summary points toward ethically transparent integration of LLM-based support tools into emergency-department workflows.

title: Audio-JEPA: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Audio Representation Learning
authors: Ludovic Tuncay; Etienne Labbé; Emmanouil Benetos; Thomas Pellegrini
principal_argument: A straightforward JEPA adaptation to audio can learn competitive audio representations without reconstructing raw waveforms.
methodology: Uses a Vision Transformer to predict masked mel-spectrogram patch representations, pretrains on unlabeled AudioSet clips, and evaluates on the X-ARES speech, music, and environmental sound suite.
main_findings: Despite being a simple translation of the original model and using no hyper-parameter tuning, Audio-JEPA is reported to perform comparably to wav2vec 2.0 and data2vec with less than one-fifth of their training data.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract presents an intentionally simple adaptation and does not detail scaling behavior, domain-specific weaknesses, or stronger audio-specialized variants.
future_opportunities: Release code and checkpoints and build more audio-specific JEPA variants for speech, music, and environmental sound tasks.

title: Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture for self-supervised pretraining on polymer molecular graphs
authors: Francesco Piccoli; Gabriel Vogel; Jana M. Weber
principal_argument: JEPA-based self-supervised pretraining can improve polymer graph learning when high-quality labels are scarce.
methodology: Applies JEPA self-supervised pretraining to polymer molecular graphs and compares downstream property-prediction performance across multiple datasets under limited-label conditions.
main_findings: The abstract reports downstream improvements across all tested datasets, with especially strong benefits when labeled data is very scarce.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary does not describe architecture choices, pretraining cost, or whether the gains persist when more labeled data is available.
future_opportunities: The work supports further use of JEPA-style graph pretraining for polymer discovery and other low-label materials-learning settings.

title: HiT-JEPA: A Hierarchical Self-supervised Trajectory Embedding Framework for Similarity Computation
authors: Lihuan Li; Hao Xue; Shuang Ao; Yang Song; Flora Salim
principal_argument: Hierarchical JEPA representations can jointly capture fine-grained and high-level trajectory semantics for better similarity computation.
methodology: Introduces a three-layer hierarchy that learns point-level, intermediate, and high-level urban trajectory representations within one joint-embedding predictive framework.
main_findings: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets are reported to yield richer multi-scale representations and improved trajectory similarity computation.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract is centered on similarity tasks and does not discuss other trajectory analytics uses or the computational trade-offs of the hierarchical design.
future_opportunities: The framework could be extended to broader urban trajectory analysis settings that benefit from multi-scale movement representations.

title: V-JEPA 2: Self-Supervised Video Models Enable Understanding, Prediction and Planning
authors: Mido Assran; Adrien Bardes; David Fan; Quentin Garrido; Russell Howes; Mojtaba; Komeili; Matthew Muckley; Ammar Rizvi; Claire Roberts; Koustuv Sinha; Artem Zholus; Sergio Arnaud; Abha Gejji; Ada Martin; Francois Robert Hogan; Daniel Dugas; Piotr Bojanowski; Vasil Khalidov; Patrick Labatut; Francisco Massa; Marc Szafraniec; Kapil Krishnakumar; Yong Li; Xiaodong Ma; Sarath Chandar; Franziska Meier; Yann LeCun; Michael Rabbat; Nicolas Ballas
principal_argument: Large-scale self-supervised video JEPA models can support world understanding, anticipation, question answering, and robotic planning from mostly observational data.
methodology: Pretrains V-JEPA 2 on over 1 million hours of internet video and images, aligns it with a large language model for video question answering, and post-trains an action-conditioned variant with limited robot data for planning.
main_findings: The abstract reports strong motion-understanding results, state-of-the-art human action anticipation and several video-QA scores, plus zero-shot pick-and-place planning on Franka arms in two labs.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not detail compute demands, failure modes, or broader robotics generalization, and the planning setup still relies on post-training with robot data.
future_opportunities: The work opens opportunities for self-supervised world models that connect web-scale video learning to physical-world planning and control.

title: seq-JEPA: Autoregressive Predictive Learning of Invariant-Equivariant World Models
authors: Hafez Ghaemi; Eilif Muller; Shahab Bakhtiari
principal_argument: Sequential JEPA world modeling can learn invariant and equivariant representations together without the usual trade-off between them.
methodology: Processes short sequences of transformed views, combines each with a relative transformation embedding, aggregates them with a transformer, and predicts the next representation conditioned on the upcoming action.
main_findings: seq-JEPA is reported to perform strongly on both invariance- and equivariance-oriented tasks and to excel on sequence-based problems such as path integration and predictive learning across eye movements.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not quantify compute or data trade-offs, and the evidence described is limited to the reported downstream tasks.
future_opportunities: The approach suggests richer sequential world models for predictive learning, navigation-like problems, and other action-conditioned perception tasks.

title: JEPA for RL: Investigating Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures for Reinforcement Learning
authors: Tristan Kenneweg; Philip Kenneweg; Barbara Hammer
principal_argument: JEPA can be adapted from self-supervised vision to reinforcement learning from images if model collapse is understood and mitigated.
methodology: Adapts JEPA to image-based reinforcement learning, studies model collapse and prevention strategies, and provides an initial demonstration on the Cart Pole task.
main_findings: The paper offers early evidence on classical Cart Pole and emphasizes practical handling of collapse in this RL setting.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract mentions only exemplary results on Cart Pole, so performance on harder RL environments and broader benchmarks is left unclear.
future_opportunities: A natural next step is evaluating JEPA-based RL on more complex image-based control tasks beyond the initial Cart Pole study.

title: SparseJEPA: Sparse Representation Learning of Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Max Hartman; Lav Varshney
principal_argument: Adding sparsity to JEPA can produce more interpretable and semantically organized representations while retaining predictive quality.
methodology: Introduces SparseJEPA with a sparsity-inducing penalty, trains a lightweight Vision Transformer on CIFAR-100, evaluates transfer with linear probes on classification and low-level tasks, and provides a theoretical argument based on reduced multiinformation.
main_findings: The abstract reports improved and more meaningful embeddings, transfer gains across task types, and theoretical support for the grouping mechanism.
limitations_or_gaps: The reported evidence is centered on CIFAR-100 and a lightweight model, leaving larger-scale behavior and broader dataset coverage unspecified.
future_opportunities: The abstract explicitly suggests extending the grouping mechanism toward new sparse and object-centric representation-learning methods.

title: JEPA4Rec: Learning Effective Language Representations for Sequential Recommendation via Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: Minh-Anh Nguyen; Dung D. Le
principal_argument: JEPA-enhanced language representation learning can improve sequential recommendation, especially when data is sparse or transfer is required.
methodology: Represents items as text sentences, encodes them with a modified bidirectional transformer, predicts masked-sentence representations, and uses a two-stage self-supervised training strategy.
main_findings: Across six real-world datasets, JEPA4Rec reportedly outperforms prior methods, with especially strong results in cross-domain, cross-platform, and low-resource scenarios.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not specify difficult recommendation cases that remain unsolved or the training cost of the two-stage pipeline.
future_opportunities: The work supports more transferable recommendation systems built from JEPA-style text representations with reduced dependence on massive pretraining corpora.

title: REJEPA: A Novel Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Efficient Remote Sensing Image Retrieval
authors: Shabnam Choudhury; Yash Salunkhe; Sarthak Mehrotra; Biplab Banerjee
principal_argument: Feature-space prediction with JEPA can make remote sensing image retrieval both more accurate and more computationally efficient.
methodology: Introduces REJEPA for unimodal remote-sensing retrieval using spatially distributed context tokens to predict target representations, with VICReg regularization to avoid collapse and promote diverse features.
main_findings: The abstract reports 40-60% lower computational complexity than pixel-reconstruction baselines and sizable retrieval gains across BEN-14K and FMoW benchmarks, with sensor-agnostic generalization.
limitations_or_gaps: The summary emphasizes benchmark gains but does not detail weak cases, modality-shift failure modes, or deployment trade-offs beyond the reported efficiency.
future_opportunities: The method is positioned as a scalable sensor-agnostic foundation for larger and more diverse remote-sensing retrieval systems.

title: TI-JEPA: An Innovative Energy-based Joint Embedding Strategy for Text-Image Multimodal Systems
authors: Khang H. N. Vo; Duc P. T. Nguyen; Thong Nguyen; Tho T. Quan
principal_argument: An energy-based JEPA pretraining strategy can reduce the semantic gap between text and image modalities in multimodal systems.
methodology: Introduces TI-JEPA, which uses an energy-based model framework for self-supervised text-image alignment and evaluates the approach on multiple multimodal benchmarks.
main_findings: The abstract reports state-of-the-art performance on multimodal sentiment analysis and suggests promise for additional tasks such as visual question answering.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract focuses on benchmark performance and does not spell out architectural details, data scale, or settings where the approach may struggle.
future_opportunities: The work suggests extending energy-based JEPA pretraining to a wider range of multimodal downstream tasks beyond sentiment analysis.

title: HEP-JEPA: A foundation model for collider physics using joint embedding predictive architecture
authors: Jai Bardhan; Radhikesh Agrawal; Abhiram Tilak; Cyrin Neeraj; Subhadip Mitra
principal_argument: A transformer-based JEPA can act as a foundation model for collider physics by learning jet representations from unlabeled constituent information and transferring well to downstream jet tasks.
methodology: Pre-trains on the 100M-jet JetClass dataset with a self-supervised JEPA-style objective that uses a subset of jet constituents as context and predicts embeddings of unseen target constituents, then evaluates on jet classification, top tagging, and quark-versus-gluon discrimination.
main_findings: The pre-trained model performs well on standard jet classification benchmarks across datasets and is evaluated competitively on top tagging and light-quark-versus-gluon discrimination against baselines and state-of-the-art high-energy-physics models.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract emphasizes jet-related benchmarks and does not detail broader collider applications, error modes, or limitations beyond the evaluated downstream tasks.
future_opportunities: Extend JEPA-style pretraining to a wider range of collider-physics tasks and study how a single pre-trained model transfers across more experimental settings.

title: ACT-JEPA: Novel Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Efficient Policy Representation Learning
authors: Aleksandar Vujinovic; Aleksandar Kovacevic
principal_argument: ACT-JEPA argues that combining imitation learning with JEPA-style self-supervision can produce more efficient policy representations and stronger world models than imitation learning alone.
methodology: Trains an end-to-end architecture that jointly predicts action sequences and latent observation sequences, using JEPA to learn in latent space across multiple environments and tasks.
main_findings: The method outperforms the strongest baseline in every tested environment, with up to 40% better world-model understanding and up to 10% higher task success rate; predicting latent observations also generalizes to predicting actions.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not specify the tested environments in detail and remains focused on the imitation-learning setting rather than broader decision-making regimes.
future_opportunities: Test the approach in more diverse control settings and further explore how latent-sequence prediction can reduce reliance on costly expert demonstrations.

title: Self-Supervised Representation Learning with Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture for Automotive LiDAR Object Detection
authors: Haoran Zhu; Zhenyuan Dong; Kristi Topollai; Beiyao Sha; Anna Choromanska
principal_argument: AD-L-JEPA proposes that JEPA-style self-supervised pretraining is a better fit for automotive LiDAR object detection than directly applying contrastive or generative methods.
methodology: Uses a non-contrastive, non-generative JEPA framework that predicts Bird's-Eye-View embeddings with explicit variance regularization, then evaluates downstream 3D object detection on KITTI3D, Waymo, and ONCE.
main_findings: The method consistently improves downstream LiDAR object detection while using 1.9x-2.7x fewer GPU hours and 2.8x-4x less GPU memory than Occupancy-MAE; on ONCE it reports 1.61 mAP and 2.98 mAP gains for 100K and 500K-frame pretraining, respectively.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract focuses on LiDAR 3D object detection and does not discuss other autonomous-driving tasks; code release is described as forthcoming rather than already available.
future_opportunities: Release the codebase, scale pretraining further, and test whether the same JEPA formulation helps additional autonomous-driving perception tasks.

title: Video Representation Learning with Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Katrina Drozdov; Ravid Shwartz-Ziv; Yann LeCun
principal_argument: VJ-VCR argues that joint-embedding predictive architectures with variance-covariance regularization can learn abstract video representations that capture scene dynamics better than generative alternatives.
methodology: Builds a self-supervised video JEPA with variance and covariance regularization to avoid collapse, compares its representations with those from a generative baseline, and explores latent variables for uncertain futures.
main_findings: Its hidden representations outperform a generative baseline on downstream tasks that require understanding the dynamics of moving objects, and the framework supports exploratory modeling of future uncertainty with latent variables.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not provide detailed quantitative results or benchmark breadth, and the latent-variable component is presented as an exploration rather than a fully settled solution.
future_opportunities: Develop stronger uncertainty-aware variants and broaden the downstream evaluation of JEPA-based video representations.

title: Learning Symmetry-Independent Jet Representations via Jet-Based Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: Subash Katel; Haoyang Li; Zihan Zhao; Raghav Kansal; Farouk Mokhtar; Javier Duarte
principal_argument: J-JEPA argues that jet representations can be learned without hand-crafted augmentations, reducing augmentation-induced bias and supporting more versatile downstream use.
methodology: Learns jet representations by predicting physical targets from an informative context in a jet-based JEPA framework, then fine-tunes the learned representations for jet tagging and compares them with task-specific alternatives.
main_findings: The work positions augmentation-free JEPA training as a route to versatile jet representations and demonstrates that the learned features can be fine-tuned for jet tagging and benchmarked against task-specific representations.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not report quantitative gains and discusses downstream evaluation mainly through jet tagging, so evidence for broader cross-task transfer is still limited at the abstract level.
future_opportunities: Evaluate the learned representations on more jet-physics tasks and pursue the proposed cross-task foundation-model direction.

title: Zero-shot Musical Stem Retrieval with Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures
authors: Alain Riou; Antonin Gagneré; Gaëtan Hadjeres; Stefan Lattner; Geoffroy Peeters
principal_argument: This paper argues that a JEPA conditioned on arbitrary instruments can enable zero-shot musical stem retrieval, especially when paired with contrastive pretraining.
methodology: Jointly trains an encoder and predictor to model context and target stems in latent space, conditions the predictor on arbitrary instruments, and validates retrieval on MUSDB18 and MoisesDB while also probing beat tracking.
main_findings: The method significantly outperforms prior baselines on both retrieval datasets, supports precise or unseen conditioning, and yields embeddings that retain temporal structure and local information on beat tracking.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract focuses on stem retrieval and a beat-tracking probe, leaving broader music-understanding or generation use cases unaddressed.
future_opportunities: Extend the conditioning scheme to more music tasks and test whether the learned embeddings support additional composition, retrieval, or analysis settings.

title: Recurrent Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture with Recurrent Forward Propagation Learning
authors: Osvaldo M Velarde; Lucas C Parra
principal_argument: A biologically inspired recurrent JEPA can learn from sequences of image fixations while avoiding representational collapse, and it can be trained without backpropagation through time via recurrent forward propagation.
methodology: Introduces a recurrent gated JEPA that predicts the next fixation representation from past fixations, provides mathematical and empirical analysis of collapse avoidance, and develops Recurrent-Forward Propagation as an exact-learning rule for a broad recurrent class.
main_findings: The paper shows mathematically and empirically that the approach avoids collapse and that Recurrent-Forward Propagation can learn efficiently while sidestepping biologically unrealistic or memory-intensive training procedures.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract explicitly says the work concentrates on theoretical innovations and leaves downstream-task evaluation and comparisons with biological vision for future work.
future_opportunities: Run downstream vision experiments and study how closely the learned representations align with biological visual processing.

title: Connecting Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture with Contrastive Self-supervised Learning
authors: Shentong Mo; Shengbang Tong
principal_argument: C-JEPA argues that combining I-JEPA with VICReg-style regularization addresses collapse and mean-prediction weaknesses in JEPA-based visual self-supervision.
methodology: Integrates image-based JEPA with variance-invariance-covariance regularization, then studies the resulting framework through theoretical and empirical evaluation including ImageNet-1K pretraining, linear probing, and fine-tuning.
main_findings: The combined method improves the stability and quality of learned visual representations and shows faster, better convergence on ImageNet-1K linear-probing and fine-tuning evaluations.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract is centered on the identified shortcomings of I-JEPA and ImageNet-1K experiments, with limited discussion of other modalities or task settings.
future_opportunities: Explore JEPA-contrastive hybrids in broader visual benchmarks and test whether the same regularization strategy helps beyond image representation learning.

title: Denoising with a Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: Dengsheng Chen; Jie Hu; Xiaoming Wei; Enhua Wu
principal_argument: D-JEPA argues that JEPA can be turned into a strong generative model by reframing it as generalized next-token prediction and coupling it with diffusion or flow-matching losses.
methodology: Reinterprets JEPA as masked image modeling for auto-regressive generation in latent space, adds diffusion loss to model per-token distributions, and also studies flow matching as an alternative objective.
main_findings: As compute increases, D-JEPA reaches lower FID with fewer epochs and its base, large, and huge variants outperform prior generative models across ImageNet conditional-generation scales.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract provides strongest evidence for ImageNet image generation, while claims about video and audio are presented as suitability rather than fully demonstrated results.
future_opportunities: Apply the framework to video and audio generation and further compare diffusion-based and flow-matching training variants.

title: M3-JEPA: Multimodal Alignment via Multi-gate MoE based on the Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: Hongyang Lei; Xiaolong Cheng; Qi Qin; Dan Wang; Kun Fan; Huazhen Huang; Qingqing Gu; Yetao Wu; Zhonglin Jiang; Yong Chen; Luo Ji
principal_argument: M3-JEPA argues that performing multimodal alignment in JEPA latent space, using a multi-gate mixture-of-experts predictor, can reduce modality collapse and improve open-world generalization.
methodology: Maps multimodal inputs into an output embedding space through a predictor implemented as a Multi-Gate Mixture of Experts, combines contrastive and regularization losses, and optimizes different multimodal tasks with alternative gradient descent.
main_findings: The framework reports state-of-the-art results across modalities and tasks, generalizes to unseen datasets and domains, and remains computationally efficient in training and inference.
limitations_or_gaps: The abstract does not break down results by task or modality in detail, and the method introduces a comparatively elaborate optimization and expert-routing setup.
future_opportunities: Use the framework as a basis for broader open-world multimodal self-supervised learning and test it on additional modality combinations and domains.

title: CNN-JEPA: Self-Supervised Pretraining Convolutional Neural Networks Using Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture
authors: András Kalapos; Bálint Gyires-Tóth
principal_argument: CNN-JEPA argues that joint embedding predictive pretraining can be adapted effectively to convolutional networks, offering a simpler and faster SSL alternative for CNNs.
methodology: Introduces a sparse CNN encoder for masked inputs, a fully convolutional predictor with depthwise separable convolutions, and an improved masking strategy, then evaluates a ResNet-50 setup on ImageNet-100.
main_findings: CNN-JEPA reaches 73.3% linear top-1 accuracy, outperforms I-JEPA with ViT architectures on ImageNet-100, and uses 17-35% less training time while approaching BYOL, SimCLR, and VICReg accuracy levels.
